title: Right Temporal-Lobe Damage
abstract:
Right
Temporal-Lobe
Damage
Perception of Unfamiliar
Stimuli After Damage
DOREEN KIMURA, Ph.D.
MONTREAL, CANADA
Milner has reported that lesions of the
right temporal lobe impair performance on
certain visual tests, whereas lesions of the
left temporal lobe do not. In a comprehen-
sive comparison of patients with damage to
the temporal, frontal, and parietal areas the
right temporal group was found to be im-
paired on a Triangular Blocks test8 and on
a complex pictorial task, the McGill Picture
Anomalies.9 The deficit on the McGill Pic-
ture Anomalies was specific to the right tem-
poral group.
Milner suggested that these
deficits are related to the nonverbal functions
of the right hemisphere and concluded that
"the right temporal lobe aids in rapid visual
identification." 9 The nature of this facilita-
tion has remained a problem, since lesions of
the right temporal lobe do not impair per-
formance on all visual tests nor exclusively
on visual tests. The present study was de-
signed
to
investigate
this
visual
deficit
further.
Subjects
The subjects were patients at the Montreal
Neurological Institute who had epileptic sei¬
zures originating from either the right or
the left temporal lobe.
In almost all cases
the abnormality was due to atrophie lesions
dating from birth
or early life.
Patients
who had tumors, diffuse damage, or epileptic
foci of doubtful origin were excluded from
the study.
Most of the patients underwent
a unilateral temporal lobectomy for the re¬
moval of epileptogenic tissue.
The small
number of cases not operated on were other¬
wise comparable to the group operated on,
and are included in the preoperative analy¬
ses. This means that the number of patients
seen on a test before operation was usually
greater than the number seen after operation.
Received for publication Oct. 8, 1962.
Read in 2 parts at Annual Meetings of the Eastern
Psychological Association, New York, April, 1960,
and the American Psychological Association, Chi-
cago, September, 1960.
From the Montreal Neurological Institute.
This work was supported by Grant 9401-11 to
Dr. D. O. Hebb from the Defence Research Board,
Ottawa, with assistance from Grant B-2831 to Dr.
Brenda Milner from the U.S. Public Health Service.

The temporal-lobe excisions typically in¬
cluded the superior, middle, and inferior
temporal gyri as well as the temporal pole,
the uncus, amygdala, and varying portions
of the hippocampus, hippocampal gyrus, and
fusiform gyrus. Measurements of the
re¬
moval cavity ranged from 5 to 7 cm. along the
Sylvian fissure, 5.5 to 9 cm. along the base, in
the right temporal lobe; 4 to 7 cm. along the
Sylvian fissure, 4 to 7 cm. along the base, in
the left temporal lobe.
The mean age and IQ for each group are
given in Table 1. These data are for all the
subjects used in this study.
Since the test
battery was being constantly modified and
extended, not all subjects were given all tests,
but any subjects who were seen were given
the complete battery as it was at the time.
The number of subjects given any one test
was therefore smaller than the number shown
in Table 1.
The actual  for each test is
given separately in the tables of results.
Materials and Methods
Tachistoscopic Tests.—In order to ascertain the
conditions under which facilitation from the right
temporal lobe might aid "rapid visual identification,"
a variety of stimuli were prepared for tachistoscopic
presentation. The general procedure for all tachisto¬
scopic tests was the same. A slide projector with
an Ilex shutter was used. Constant illumination was
maintained by having
one projector illumine the
screen and another project the image. The light in¬
tensity of the
2 projectors
was the
same.
The
projector which
illumined the
screen
was
auto¬
matically shut off during presentation of the image.
The stimulus material
was prepared
on cards,
photographed, and presented black-on-white on 35
mm. slides. Where a fixation point was used, it was
projected by the preexposure projector and was,
therefore, on the screen at all times, except when
the stimuli appeared.
The subject was seated 7 feet from the screen
with the tachistoscope directly behind him.
The
light from the machine projected onto an area of
white screen, 3 feet by 2]/¿ feet. There was no other
light in the room.
Since the experimenter operated
the machine from the back, manipulation of slides,
recording of responses, and timing of responses were
all done outside the subject's range of vision.
A ready signal
was given immediately before
each slide was projected, to enable the subject to
fixate the dot in the center of the screen or simply
to fixate the center of the screen when no dot was
used.
One or 2 practice slides were given before
each test.
In all tests except Letter Recognition,
different but similar sets of stimuli were used before
and after operation.
Since subjects with lesions of the dominant hemi¬
sphere sometimes had difficulty postoperatively in
naming the objects they saw, a recognition test was
used in those situations in which objects were pre¬
sented. The Nonsense Figures test was also given
by the recognition method. The subject was given a
card containing all the stimuli presented during that
test,
and he made his
responses from the card
either by saying the number of the object or by
pointing it out.
The measure of performance used
throughout
was the number of stimuli correctly
recognized.
The 5 tachistoscopic tests in the order
in which they were given are described in Table 2,
and illustrations of the material are shown in Figure
1. Immediately before giving the Overlapping Non¬
sense Figures test, the subject was presented with
4 single nonsense figures for 10 msec. each.
For
the Dots test, the subject simply reported how many
dots he had seen. The Familiar Objects were photo¬
graphs cut from magazines, pasted on cards, and
photographed again for reproduction on slides. They
are not shown.
Recurring Figures.—Because there is impairment
in verbal learning and verbal memory in patients
with lesions of the dominant temporal lobe, and in

Recurring Figures.—Because there is impairment
in verbal learning and verbal memory in patients
with lesions of the dominant temporal lobe, and in
Table 2.—Description of Tachistoscopic Tests
Fig. 1.—Examples from tachistoscopic tests: A, letters; B, overlapping nonsense figures; C,
overlapping familiar figures ; D, dots.
test: A, geometric design; B, nonsense design.
line with Milner's " suggestion that the left and right
temporal areas may have functions related to verbal
and nonverbal abilities respectively, it seemed prob¬
able that the right temporal lobe would play a role
in the learning and retention of nonverbal material.
Consequently a visual learning task was devised in
which certain patterns were repeatedly presented to
the subject interspersed with other nonrecurring
designs. This is the Recurring Figures test described
The subject was shown a series of cards, each card
containing a line drawing of either a regular geo¬
figure (Fig. 2B). After a short practice series, the
subject was presented with 20 cards for approxi¬
mately 3 seconds each. Eight of the designs on these
cards (4 nonsense, 4 geometric)
again later ; the other 12 were not. After this initial
trial, 7 more trials of 20 cards each were presented.
The 8 recurring designs appeared randomly in each
series, along with 12 new designs (half of them
nonsense, half geometric). This meant that an inter¬
val of 30 to 90 seconds might elapse between suc¬
cessive presentations of the same design.
To each of the 140 cards, the subject was
quired to say "yes" if he thought he had
before or "no" if he thought he had not
Table 3.—Mean Scores on Tachistoscopic Tests
before. Of the 140 figures, 56 were recurring; they
consisted of the 8 drawings of the first trial pre¬
The number of correct recognitions of the recur¬
ring figures, minus the number of false positives,
yields the net score used. This test was given after
The right temporal group was inferior to
the left on 3 tests—Overlapping Nonsense
Figures, Dots, and Recurring Figures. The
results are given in detail below.
Tachistoscopic Tests (Tables 3 and 4).—
There is no significant difference between
the 2 temporal-lobe groups, either pre- or
postoperatively, on the Letters test or on the
Overlapping Familiar Figures test. On Fa¬
miliar Objects the right temporal group ac¬
temporal group postoperatively (/><0.04).
On the Overlapping Nonsense Figures test,
however, the right temporal group is signifi¬
cantly inferior to the left temporal group
both before (/><0.01) and after (/><0.02)
operation. A comparison between the groups
on the single nonsense figures presented for
practice yielded no significant difference, al¬
though the trend was in the same direction,
i.e., the right temporal group was slightly
less efficient than the left. On the Dots test
also, the right temporal group is significantly
inferior to the left (/><0.001 pre, /><0.04
Analysis of the tachistoscopic data, then,
indicates that the right temporal group is im¬
paired, relative to the left temporal group,
on 2 tasks—Nonsense Figures and Dots. In
contrast, these subjects perform at least as
well as the left temporal group on the
group of patients with frontal-lobe lesions
(whose data are not shown) indicates that
there is no difference between the frontal-
lobe group and either temporal-lobe group
on the 3 tests in Table 3. The lack of a dif¬
ference between the 2 temporal-lobe groups
is, therefore, not due to the fact that both
sulted in contralateral homonymous visual
field defects of varying size but rarely in¬
volving more than an upper quadrant.
jects with field defects larger than an upper
quadrant were not included in these analyses.
It is still possible that field defects
somewhat larger in the right-sided group
than in the left. However, deficits present
before the operation are obviously not at¬
tributable to field defects, and when one con¬
shown by the right temporal group was re-

stricted to the 2 tests on which they were
impaired before operation, it becomes clear
that possible differences in size of field de¬
fects is not the cause of the impairment.

Recurring Figures (Tables 5 and 6).—It
is clear that the right temporal group is
inferior to the left on the Recurring Figures
combined score (Table 5) is highly signifi¬
cant (/><0.001). Even when one considers
the scores for nonsense figures and geometric
forms separately, the differences are signifi¬
When these scores were analyzed further,
it became apparent that the difference be¬
tween the groups was due to the large num¬
ber of incorrect "yes" responses made by the
right temporal group. This is shown in Table
The difference between groups in the
number of false positives is of course signifi¬
cant (p<0.00l). Total correct refers to the
number of recurring figures to which the
appropriate response "yes" was made, before
any correction for false positives. The groups
do not differ on this measure. The number
of false positives, therefore, accounts almost
entirely for the difference between groups
The average size of the excision on the
right is larger than on the left, although there
is a great deal of overlap. However, within
the large group of 31 left temporal patients
there is no relation between score on this test
with the smallest excisions on the right and
the 9 patients with the largest excisions on
the left, so that the 2 groups are matched for
size of removal, the mean scores are very
similar to those in Table 5, and the difference
between the groups is still significant. Thus
the difference cannot be accounted for on the
seen 2 weeks after operation. Some data are
available also for a small group of patients
who were given this test for the first time
a year or more after operation. Six patients
with excisions of the right temporal lobe had
excisions of the left temporal lobe had a mean
score of 37.0. The difference between these
scores is not significant, probably because of
the small N, but the means are clearly com¬
It is apparent that subjects with lesions
of the right temporal lobe are impaired in the
perception of overlapping nonsense figures
and of groups of dots. They are not impaired
on other tachistoscopic tasks, all of which
utilize familiar material. This is despite the
Table 6.—Analysis of Scores on Recurring Figures
fact that both types of material were pre¬
sented visually and under closely similar
material that is still well perceived after right
temporal-lobe damage and material that is
not well perceived seems to be the degree of
familiarity. The nonsense figures are obvi¬
ously unfamiliar material, and although dots
themselves are common stimuli, the enumera¬
tion of groups of dots does not depend on
the recognition of any familiar configura¬
The fact that unilateral temporal-lobe dam¬
age impairs the perception of unfamiliar vis¬
ual material, but not of familiar material,
suggests that the familiar material is better
represented in terms of number of neural
or in the efficiency of those units.
Hebb3 (p. 293) has suggested that in the
initial establishment of a "cell assembly," the
neural correlate for a percept, more cells are
necessary than for its functioning after it
has been established. According to this view,
the unfamiliar material, having no permanent
mode of transmission, is more sensitive to
of brain tissue than the familiar. The
further finding that right temporal-lobe dam¬
age impairs the perception of the unfamiliar
material, whereas left temporal-lobe damage
does not, indicates that the right temporal
lobe is more important than the left in the
establishing of such cell assemblies.
There is, however, another important dif¬
ference between the familiar and unfamiliar
tachistoscopic material. This is the degree of
verbal identifiability. On an a priori basis,
it seems clear that a frequent (though not a
necessary) concomitant of familiarity in a
perceptual sense is the possibility of verbal
with a stimulus object, or class of objects, is
associated with the repeated naming of the
object, the ability instantly to attach a name
to it represents an important step in the de¬
velopment of a concept. It seems probable
that in such cases a large part of the increase
in permanent neural representation which is
assumed to correlate with familiarity will
take place in language centers, that is, in the
familiarity is not associated with repeated
verbal identification, the permanent neural
representation of such stimuli need not be
extensively elaborated in the dominant hemi¬
It thus appears that the 2 hemispheres do
not play equal parts in the perception of all
kinds of material. The right temporal lobe
is evidently more important for the percep¬
tion of unfamiliar visual material than is the
left temporal lobe. On the other hand, studies
on object agnosia after parieto-occipital dam¬
age suggest that recognition of familiar ob¬
trend for the left temporal group of the pres¬
ent study to be inferior to the right in the
recognition of familiar objects would be con¬
sistent with this point of view. It should be
kept in mind, however, that in the majority
of cases of object agnosia, there is evidence
of damage to the right side of the brain as
well as the left.2,4 This is apparently not so
for many cases of alexia, where it seems that
the recognition of visually presented words
can be affected by lesions restricted to the
dominant hemisphere, and in the absence of
other forms of aphasia. These facts suggest
that, as material becomes more verbal, its per¬
ception depends more on the dominant hemi¬
sphere, since the final identification of the
material is more intimately bound up with
the centers for speech. This notion receives
support from the fact that familiar objects
curately perceived in the right visual field
than in the left, but geometric designs,1 non¬
sense figures,5·14 and groups of dots 13 are
The explanation suggested here is that the
right temporal lobe, perhaps with other parts
of the right hemisphere, participates in the
immediate postexposure trace for all types
of tachistoscopic stimuli, since it does not
seem reasonable to suppose that familiar and
temporal-lobe damage does not affect the
recognition of familiar tachistoscopic mate-
The reason for this may be that the
identification of familiar stimuli is more de¬
pendent on neural connections not available
to the unfamiliar stimuli, connections which
may, as suggested above, be primarily with
An objection to the stimulus-familiarity
hypothesis may be made on the grounds that
damage to the right temporal lobe also im¬
Anomalies test which depicts common objects
and familiar situations. The case here, how¬
ever, is quite different. It is not simply a
question of recognizing individual objects ;
meaningful scene in which the separate ob¬
jects are only units. This is essential to good
performance on the task, since in order to
pick out the incongruous object,
Similarly, the identification of various fea¬
tures of the Mooney Closure Faces, which
is impaired after right temporal lobectomy,6
requires more than the simple recognition of
a face as such. It is unlikely that activity cor¬
responding to these complex situations can
be so easily converted into verbal concepts
as the identification of a single object may
That is, the substitution of pathways
other than those dependent on the right tem¬
poral lobe would be much harder to effect.
Thus it is conceivable that, whereas the rec¬
ognition of common objects could take place
without facilitation from the right temporal
lobe, this area would still be necessary for
the efficient perception of complex pictures.
Memory and the Right Temporal Lobe
The right temporal group was significantly
inferior to the left on the Recurring Figures
test, a task requiring the recognition of pre¬
viously presented visual designs. This is a
particularly striking finding in view of the
impairment shown by the left temporal group
on the learning of paired associates, both
right temporal group performs well. It em¬
phasizes again the importance of the non¬
The finding that patients with right tem¬
poral-lobe excisions give more false recogni¬
tions of unfamiliar material was unexpected.
That this is not due simply to a general
tendency to make false recognitions is in¬
dicated by 2 facts. One is the deficit in the
identification of nonsense figures presented
despite the fact that there was no penalty
for making false recognitions. The second
piece of evidence comes from an auditory
recurring task employing the presentation of
various birdsongs, some repeated, some not.
On this test there is no difference in net
score between the 2 temporal-lobe groups,
and the right temporal group has no more
false positives than the left.
difficulty which the subject has in discrim¬
inating between stimuli he has encountered
before and those he has not encountered will
It might be argued, from the impairment
nonsense figures presented tachisto¬
scopically, that the deficit on the recurring
figures task is not a retention deficit but a
perceptual one. Data from the tachistoscopic
presentation of single nonsense figures, how¬
ever, indicate that the recognition of this
material immediately after presentation is
unimpaired. With reference to the geometric
designs, Milner 8 reported no deficit in the
right temporal group on the Benton Visual
Memory Test in which geometric figures had
to be reproduced immediately after presenta¬
tion. The same is true for the immediate re¬
production of the Visual Designs subtest of
the Wechsler Memory Scale, although de¬
layed recall of these figures is affected.11
One cannot, therefore, consider the deficit on
Recurring Figures as a deficit in perception
in the usual sense of the word for either the
geometric or the nonsense designs.
This does not mean, however, that the
"retention" deficit on the Recurring Figures
is unrelated to the "perceptual" deficit on the
tachistoscopically. It is quite probable that
the initial trace-activity corresponding to un-
familiar visual stimuli is defective after right
temporal-lobe damage. If the task is kept
relatively simple, as is done in the tachisto¬
scopic presentation of the single
figures, this defect may not be apparent.
Similarly, in a paired associates learning task
employed by Meyer,7 the extreme simplicity
of the designs, some of which approximated
common objects, may account for the lack
of deficit after right temporal lobectomy. It
is only when the demands on the trace-ac¬
tivity are increased, either by complicating
the stimulus or by inserting a time interval
between presentation and recognition of the
stimulus, that the defect appears. Thus the
initial trace-activity may not be so defective
as to be apparent in immediate reproduction,
but the defect will manifest itself in subse¬
quent recall. What the additional role of the
hippocampus on one side may be in the "con¬
solidation" 10 of these traces is a question to
which the data of the present study do not
were presented tachistoscopically to patients
with lesions of the right or the left temporal
lobe. The right temporal group was impaired
in the perception of the unfamiliar stimuli
requiring the recognition of previously pre¬
sented unfamiliar designs, the right temporal
discussed in terms of the respective roles of
the dominant and nondominant hemispheres
in the perception of verbal and nonverbal
